findings.message: `T004` and ADR 0003 require four contradiction warnings (`add+baseline`, `modify+no-baseline`, `reference+no-baseline`, `reference+not-blue`), but the acceptance criteria only lock down the first one. That means an implementation can omit three required warning paths and still satisfy the approved spec.

The spec gives conflicting direction on compatibility: line 112 frames omitted `action` as a backward-compatibility requirement, while line 137 and ADR 0003 explicitly say backward compatibility is not supported for pre-TDDD-03 tracks. This ambiguity matters for `T006`, because the codec contract is underspecified in two incompatible ways.

The `skip_serializing_if=add` requirement is sourced to ADR 0003, but that ADR never specifies encode-time omission; it only covers optional/default `action`. Because this provenance is wrong, both the scope item here and the matching acceptance criterion are no longer traceable to their stated source.

The planner note still says the codec change is for "backward-compatible JSON", but ADR 0003 §Consequences and this track's spec now explicitly say completed-track backward compatibility is out of scope. Because `spec.json` cites this planner section as a source, the provenance is internally contradictory and can mislead implementation toward migration behavior the ADR rejected.

The acceptance criteria never require a happy-path check for explicit `modify`/`reference`/`delete` action values surviving codec round-trip and behaving correctly when the declaration matches reality. Right now the new criteria cover default/add serialization and contradiction/error paths, so TDDD-03 could be marked complete without proving most of the new public `action` variants actually work.

`updated_at` is still `2026-04-11T12:23:13Z`, but this track's `spec.json` was re-approved later at `2026-04-11T12:39:33Z`. The track workflow falls back to `metadata.json.updated_at` to resolve the latest materialized track, so leaving this stale can misorder or skip this track in `track/status` and non-track `track/commit` flows.

EC-7 says duplicate action is "handled by existing name uniqueness", but this track now requires a codec-level exception that accepts exactly one same-name `delete` + `add` pair. That design note is now inconsistent with the spec/metadata for T006 and can mislead implementation of the duplicate-name validation change.

The acceptance criteria still lack an explicit happy-path check for `action: delete` surviving the codec boundary and reaching the delete forward-check path. Right now the spec covers default/add omission plus modify/reference round-trips and delete error handling, so T006/T002 could still be marked complete without proving the new `delete` variant works end to end.

`metadata.json` broadens the approved TDDD-03 scope with `T011` (`catalogue.rs` split into three modules), but neither the ADR, planner review, nor `spec.json` define that refactor as part of this track. Adding unscoped work here means the generated plan now requires an unrelated structural change before the feature is complete, which creates review churn and can delay delivery of the actual action-declaration behavior.

`schema_version: 3` track metadata requires each task object to carry `{id, description, status, commit_hash}`, but this edit removes `commit_hash` from every task. That breaks the documented SSoT shape for `metadata.json` and will prevent per-task completion provenance from being recorded once tasks transition to `done`.

`TypeAction::Delete` on a name that is not in the baseline only appends to `delete_errors` here, while the delete entry still suppresses group-4 undeclared detection for that name. That means an invalid `delete` declaration can produce no Red signals at all and will be silently accepted by existing `ConsistencyReport` consumers unless they are updated to consult the new field.
